title: 2025/12/04 - PP (Part 4)
displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Primary Production Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries [Part 4]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Primary Production Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries Hering of evidence: Hon Nicola Grigg, Associate Minister of Agriculture (Horticulture)
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries. Hering of evidence. Hon Nicola Grigg, Associate Minister of Agriculture (Horticulture)

chunks.text: All right, we've been studying. I like studying. You've been studying? Are you ready for my questions about... Performance measures? Stuff. (laughs) That stuff. Women in horticulture. (laughs) (laughs) Oh, yeah. Don't do that again. (laughs) (laughs) I actually don't have any?????? (laughs) Yeah. Let's go. A-billions for apple growers.

Right. Nearly there. Welcome, Minister. Yeah. (laughs) Uh, thanks for being, uh, nimble enough to come five minutes early. Um, so we've got you for half an hour, um... Goodness. We leave it in your hands. So, fire away and then, uh, as you know, the committee will be bursting at the seams- Boing. ... to ask questions.

I wouldn't expect anything else. Look, thank you, um, m- members for your time. I appreciate you've had a long week and you've had a particularly long morning. So, look, I'll keep my introductory mark, remarks short. I know that the two beside me need no introduction, so I'll just kick into it. Look, um, suffice to say, the government is absolutely right behind our $60 billion food and fiber sector, and we really are working hard to ensure their ongoing success.

But I'm here to talk to you today specifically about horticulture, uh, being the first minister with that dedicated responsibility, um, for that sector. And because of that, it's very much my job to, uh, ensure that our work program as a government supports our fruit and veggie growers around the country. And so I do want to take the opportunity to acknowledge and recognize and thank them for the huge role that they play, uh, in, in New Zealand's, um, economic recovery.

And it is thanks to their very, very hard work that we are seeing rising export revenues. Um, economic growth is starting to occur in the regions, so very much boosting our, our national and regional economies, uh, and feeding not just New Zealanders, but millions and millions of people around the world. Um, so that does need to be acknowledged.

Um, the numbers don't lie. It really is an incredible success story in horticulture at the moment. When Ray launched the SOPE and took all the credit for it back in June, we were looking at a 19% growth for the horticulture sector. It's actually, the revised number is 25%. Um, so that- Wow.... is huge, $8.8 billion in exports in the year- (laughs)... to June, uh, 30. Um, and of course, apples and kiwifruit very much took center stage. They've both, um, posted record numbers. Kiwifruit particularly have reached four billion.

Um, that is quite incredible because it had naturally surpassed three billion previously. Um, that is driven by a 30% increase in export volumes. And then apples and pears also performed very, very strongly. They posted numbers for the first time over a billion, at $1.2 billion, uh, again thanks to, um, rising export volumes and, and indeed prices. They really are extraordinary results and that is why I just wanted to take a, a couple of, a minute to touch on it. But they do show very much, uh, the hard work and resilience of those

growers. And I know members at this table, um, come from regions that have been particularly hard-hit by, uh, incredible weather events. And so just acknowledging the work that, that you, uh, as local MPs have done to represent those growers. Um, as a government, we're very much supporting the, uh, uh, Horticulture Action Plan, the Aotearoa Horticulture Action Plan. That has been pulled together by the industry for the industry. Um, its aim, um, coincidentally, happens to be to, to double, uh, export revenue by 2035,

which as you'll all know is also the government's targeted goal. Um, the first phase of that roadmap, uh, identifies 52 actions over the next three years that will really be critical to, to bolstering that growth. Uh, and the government, for its part, has to take responsibility for 21 of those actions.

Uh, and they include, um, areas like supporting the trade and market access opportunity, removing all the regulatory barriers that we've been hearing about for a number of years, um, to achieve that growth. Uh, and then of course, what's really critical is the pragmatic on-farm support. So, um, enabling better access to chemicals and building the capability of that crucial workforce.

Um, and just very quickly, I also do have, happily have the delegated responsibility for rural women. So I'm really, really proud to back the efforts of women in our food and fiber sector. I know we like to talk about farmers being the backbone of our economy. Well, I would argue that it's actually the women farmers that are the backbone of our economy. Um, so the government's provided Rural Women New Zealand in their centenary year with a quarter of a million dollars to, to help boost them with their, uh, on the ground support for their rural communities.

Um, they're using that funding to roll out a couple of initiatives. One particularly is called ConnectHer, which is a project that's been rolled out in Wairoa on the East Coast and also in Southland to connect rural women and of course their families, um, with those services that will help them support their economic and social, mental and physical wellbeing, and to help reduce the isolation that a lot of those, um, communities do feel.

Um, there's also a number of initiatives we're backing, um, that foster leadership and entrepreneurship. Uh, that includes the Activator Programme which, which Ray has very kindly funded, uh, which is run by rural women. Uh, it's been, been co-funded since 2019 and it helps to turn some what start out as very niche ideas to help grow them into viable businesses, again, to help support those rural communities. And we've also committed, Ms.

Boyack- (laughs)... you'll be pleased to know, $300,000 over three years to that (laughs) - (laughs) Excellent. ... to that United Fresh WomenHort initiative, which she so- Excellent.... kindly reminded us of last time. (laughs) This is what Ray does. He just writes checks and forgets about them. Um, but look, very much, um, here to, to take your questions. So I'll just close off here, but, um, I just want to really reiterate that, that, um, our food and fibre sector is absolutely integral to the economy, to this country. Um, for our, for my part as a minister, indeed for our part

as a government, we've, we've been really careful to really listen and, and focus on, on what truly matters and then to, um, create policy that enables that growth. And so look, with that, thank you very much for your time. Very happy to take all your questions. Very good, into questions. Who wants to go first? Yeah. Okay. I'll go f-, I'll go. Thank you, um, I had a couple lined up for you and you answered them in your intro, so- (laughs) ... I was thinking, "Oh, what I'll ask." But no, um- Maybe to try again.

Yeah. I've got a couple in mind for you. So, it's, um... Uh, thank you for your time this afternoon too and thank you, um, to the MPI team who are here again as well. It's been a long day for you, I know. So, um, right. I just wanted to ask you some questions with regard to the roadmap- Mm-hmm.... that you, um, unveiled, um, in conjunction with the industry. So, I'm keen to know, um, you've, you've talked about some of the support that the government's putting into helping the in- industry double its exports by 2035. Um, you talk about

it being industry driven, the roadmap being industry driven. What other support then is the government taking responsibility for is, in, in that space, if anything at all, whether financial or just in the way of support? Um, like I said, um, it... The success of this roadmap will very much depend on industry getting involved across its entirety. And of course you'll appreciate there's enormous sectors within the sector, like kiwi and apple, and then there's the smaller persimmons, for example, and then there growing and emerging industries like

blueberry and cherry. Um, so, so for our part, um, we have to do... We have to look at the, at the bigger picture. And so the... Of those 21 actions that, that, uh, down to government responsibility, enabling water storage, absolutely critical, um, and as I said before, support in that trade and market access. There's no point growing those products if there's nowhere- Mm-hmm.

... to send them off to. Um, and then as I said, the better access to new chemicals, which, um, very much sits under, um, Minister Hager's responsibility now, um, but he has, um, you know, got the EPA organized, and sorry, the Minister for the Environment's got the EPA organized. And they have been able to, I think, uh, deliver, improve the backlog by about 30% to enable more access to chemicals for those particular growers. That's just a couple of them.

Sure. Okay. Can I ask something on that one? Yes. Me go. Yeah. So, of those 20, uh, one government actions, so, so to, uh, speak, um, have you got a timeline of which you are trying to achieve getting those barriers out of the way? I'm gonna have to refer to the officials on that. So, each of the actions have got their own, um, own, own timeline. Um, uh, Tim might have a copy of the action plan and go through, but there's some stuff.

A lot of it's, um, aligned with existing government programs, so like, um, the labor market, um, settings, um, access to RAC workers. so, uh, and that will be lined up with the, the work that the government's, uh, already got and it's making sure that the horticulture voice and the horticulture's- (clears throat)... um, priorities are actually, uh, properly integrated and heard as those policies go forward.

So for example, the market access area is an area that doesn't... It doesn't really stop, so what we'll do is we'll work with the Plant Market Access Council, um, we'll get... We'll work with them to identify what the market access priorities are for the horticulture sector, um, and, and there'll be a prioritization process on depending on how ready they are to enter the market, the analysis of the market in terms of the demand and whether, um, there's any competing, um, country that's trying to get those goods in. Does New Zealand have a, a, an

advantage on that? And based on that, that'll be used to prioritize that. And then we'll work with that sector, um, to get access, um, for those goods into those markets. So we've had some success in Vietnam. I think we're working on Pekaboo pears at the moment, going into the Vietnam market. And so there'll be priorities for each of that.

So once we've knocked off Peka- Pekaboo pears, there'll be another, um, uh, variety that will then come through and fill that space. Just another sub if you don't mind, Mr. Chair. Um, so, uh, a, a number of the, uh, actions would be a policy, um, bureaucracy, getting that sort of...
What, what about the ones that are potentially got, um, large amounts of capital required towards it, such as you talked about water storage and the- Yeah.... yeah, what, what's, what's the

... um, we've been working with them, um, as, to help them build a plan about how they grow their sector and how they meet some of the, the challenging areas around nutrient losses and so on, and, and what can we do to help invest with them to help them sort of overcome some of those challenges. And as Julie said, there will be opportunities in various markets.

So I know, I know with the Chinese at the moment, I was there last week, we are talking to them about onion axis into, into the China market to help diversify. So we're quite exposed just into one market into Indonesia. So we can, we can begin to do those things. So I think with that, under that horticultural action plan, what we're trying to do is help that sector grow and it's the small, small parts of the sector that are more domestically focused as well as the, as the bigger, the fruit parts are kind of quite well established like kiwifruit, apples and others.

Thank you. Jo? Thank you. Um, coming back to the roadmap, um, Minister, I'm interested to know what criteria will be used to define special agricultural areas that you've referred to, um, uh, in media commentary around the, um, the roadmap.

So that was consulted on as part of the 20th Plan for Land consultations. Yes. You go. Uh- Either or. Yeah, but I was going to say exactly that. But, um, um, yes, as, as you'll be aware, the government's indicated that, um, we can, intend to remove land use class 3, um, from the HPL, uh, MPS and HPL, um, but where, except for where there is a natural grouping.

Um, it is still, uh, you know, we're obviously yet to see, um, the, the new Resource Management Act presented to the Parliament. Um, one would, um, one can, I think, confidently predict that within that, there will be, uh, national direction around what those designation of those special agricultural areas will look like.

Mm. Steve from Renewal? Yep. He's just (whispers) . Um, good to have you here. Hello. Um, we received submissions on the, uh, Plant Health Environment Capability Facility from various parties, and Horticulture New Zealand, uh, submitted they support the, uh, facility being built, but they're concerned about the import costs on germplasm. Um, MPI has initiated plant import system reform project, um, and HortNZ are part of, an active collaborator, they

say. But they also say, "We believe this review, um, is necessary as the current system is slow, costly and stifles innovation." Mm-hmm. "For maximum benefit, the review should take a wide scope." And they go on to say that, "Import systems cost structure are worth revisiting to improve affordability for the industry while maintaining high biosecurity standards, um, and that significant fee increases may make importation undesirable, particularly for smaller sectors." So I wonder how much you're across the, um, the concerns

in the importation of novel germplasm? Yeah. Look, um, I, I will throw to Ray 'cause it is very much operational and not actually my ministerial delegation. However, um, it is a, a key concern that is raised with me regularly by- Right. ... the horticultural industry. Uh, and so I guess m- my job in, in that instance is to convey those concerns to those that run the operations, um, and, and see where we can strike a better balance.

Yeah, I mean, we talked about it a little bit earlier today, but- Yeah, we talked about the facility quite a lot. It was- Yeah, yeah. And, and I guess the, the, this is, this is the sort of challenge in this is, um, the cost. A- in order to bring the material in, you've got to have a facility. Yep. Uh, in order to build a facility, it costs money.

Then you got to figure out who's benefiting from this. So there's no doubt a benefit, uh, at a taxpayer level generally, but not 100%. And so we, we've struck a new fee structure that says, well, it is going to cost more, cost more than what they used to pay.

Uh, and so, you know, that hasn't, not everyone's thrilled with that, right? Um, but some- somebody's got to pay for it and it can't just be taxpayers. So, so however, I think one of the things that we need to work a bit harder at, um, I went and had a look at the facility the other day and these new glass houses, and there's more to come. Uh, the way they're structured now, you don't, you don't just have to hire a plant for your imported asparagus stalk. You can mix that with a range of common genuses and, and therefore you can reduce the cost.

And I think these, there are some clever things we can do that actually can make it, um, more viable. And the other thing is, we don't think in all product types we have to have them in quarantine for as long as we used to. So some we will, but not all, not all the types. So we think there's ways of actually reducing the cost, but there's- Sharing space. Yeah, sharing space. There's just not enough information I think out there at the moment. And there's, look, there's been some strong and hard lobbying by those who, who have imported the most

that have taken up most of the space. Mm-hmm. Uh, and they've, they've been able to bring product in at a very low cost. But there are a range of people that are using the facilities at the moment. We just, I think had to build a bit of confidence with it. I think it's a bit of a challenge on us to get out there and show people what can be done and, and to try to reduce the cost. On the import health standards stuff, that is, you know, I think we all see it as... One area we think is going to help us with that is, is the use of AI, but we will have to go carefully with

that. So if you wanted to import a new varietal-... and we had never done the study on what diseases might manifest themselves in these varietals, uh, internationally, then scientists could spend two years researching, um, sort of like the pig research we were talking about.

You know? (laughs) Research and then trying to form a view about the likelihood of those things coming and being present. With AI tools, that part of the job probably can be cleaned up very, very quickly and you come down to a lesser amount of analysis that would need to be done. So, we think there are some, some real opportunities with technology to kind of help speed up that process, that, that will take the price out.

Thank you. Very good. Yep. Thank you, uh, Mr. Chair. Um, uh, coming back to the, um, plan of doubling the e- sector's exports in 20, by 2035, um, the high, the roadmap high, high-level plan-... to achieve that target talks about ensuring that Maori are strong in horticulture and I'm interested to know what engagement you've had with Maori in the horticultural sector and what you're doing as minister to support that.

I've had a fair amount of engagement. I've visited, uh, a number of Maori growers. I've actually traveled overseas on various trade missions with the Prime Minister and a number of Maori horticulturalists. Um, there, as you'll know, there is a dedicated, uh, Maori business unit within MPI, um, and, and, you know, the roadmap itself identifies the massive potential for Maori horticulturalists.

Mm. And so it stands to reason that the government would work alongside those growers. Mm. Uh, and, you know, the likes of KANO, as and where it is, uh, uh, appropriate and applicable, um, we encourage them to, to take advantage of those opportunities to help develop, um, things. You know, from, from small ideas and concepts to, to, um, multinational businesses at times. Um, so yeah, there's incredible opportunity, but, you know, we're, we are really, really dedicated to, to supporting that sector within the sector.

Thank you. Sup? Yeah. Well, well sort of, but- All right.... kind of related. Related to, related to horticulture (crosstalk) Yeah.... might be a sup anyway. But, um, uh, with regard to the different, um, uh, the different groups within the horticultural sector that, um, that you referred to as fragmented, not in a, in a negative way.

I didn't use that word. Not in a negative way, but you know, in a, in a... Because there's so many different moving parts and so many different groups within the hort sector. Yeah. Mm-hmm. Um, what are you, what are you doing to support the coordination of all of them to sort of come together and, and, um, uh, work more closely together for the benefit of the whole of the sector?

Well, it, it's a fine balancing act. Again, I, I don't see that I have a ministerial responsibility for that. Having said that, you know, we're the sum of all our parts, right? And so you want every single, um, sub-sector within the sector to be doing well. Uh- Mm.... I think one of the very small, uh, initiatives that, that I have established is, is just a regular quarterly update to every single grower group- Yeah. ... in the country.

Yeah. Just to, um, identify and inform them of every single government policy that- Mm.... that we all here are pushing through that Parliament. Because so often things get lost and people don't actually understand- Mm.... what has changed and, and what opportunities are in front of them. Yeah. So, I know it seems like a very small initiative, but it's actually been received really, really well.

Yeah. Yeah. Because thus far, government hadn't really been communicating with all parts of the sector. But- Yeah.... you know, then I would also throw to the responsibility of those grower groups. Mm. Um, particularly those who, you know, who pay their commodity levies. Uh, um, and so, you know, they have an obligation to be- best represent their growers. But look, I can't speak highly enough of Horticulture New Zealand.

Mm. I think they do an exceptional job at uniting a, you know, a vast array- Yeah.... of growers across the country, in sizes and shapes and all sorts of different products. Uh, and I often say to grower groups, "You are really well-represented by this organization." Mm. And, you know, and we're only as good as the information that we get given.

So, so I think HortNZ particularly are very, very good conduit between those, those grassroots growers and, and us as government. Yeah. Do you think that there's more opportunity, or could be potential for more opportunity if they, um, were to perhaps all sort of come together and coordinate a little bit more? You know, sort of like power in numbers, so to speak.

Do you think? Well, it would depend on, on what their objective is. Um, you know, what, what I would always do would be encourage growers to, to look to the vast array of trade arrangements that we're beginning to establish across the country- Mm.... across the gro- globe. And, you know, where there are, um, opportunities to fill quotas and so on and so forth- Mm.

... then, then I do encourage them to, to band together- Yeah.... create volume. Uh, but, you know, it, it is up to them. There's... I suppose there's an ideological argument to have between how much the long arm of government reaches into people's lives and into their businesses. But our, our job is, is to free up the regulations and the restrictions to enable them to go and, you know, make, make what they want to of, of the opportunity in front of them.

Mm. Thank you. Very good. Further questions? No. Well, it looks like we've finished. Very good. That came from Thank you, Minister.... at the night facts in Canterbury. (laughs) Come on, Steve Avic. Thank you. I'll take further questions. (laughs) (laughs) Um, how, how much less does the horticulture I was joking. (laughs) (laughs) ... um, in terms of water pollution? It's a good question because, um...

(laughs) (laughs) Thank you. Me and my big mouth. I thought we'd finished. So had Oh, no. The minister wanted another question. So- (laughs) The, the horticultural sector is a obvious means of us producing more direct human food from plant products.

Mm-hmm. That is part of what is recognized globally as a solution to the big emissions component of livestock farming, and the big pollution component- Mm-hmm.... from nitrates, as you allude to, which primarily comes from dairy (00:03:00) KU run. Is that seen by the government as an opportunity? If we could make farm gate profits from a hectare of horticultural activity, and it might displace livestock activity, you haven't reduced the country's productivity, you've changed the nature of production.

Mm-hmm. And we did have, we do have AgriZero where we're investing lots of money- That's right. ... in tech fixes- Mm-hmm.... but not doing the most known recognized means to affect the emissions profile livestock, which has moved to more plant-based production.

So, the big boost in horticulture that you've, you know, outlined for us today, does the government say, "Great. This helps us also meet our climate targets"? I, I, I will always maintain that it's an and/and, that we can produce low emissions protein and we can produce low emissions fruit and vegetables that are good for our people and good for the world and good for the environment. I think we as a government take the view that land use change is market driven. Um, pri-, you know, providing it will, it falls within essential environmental

imperatives. But you are starting to see, and I can talk to Canterbury particularly, um, that land use change naturally occurring. Just recently I visited, um, a converted dairy farm that's going into 250 hectares of apples. Mm-hmm. That's market driven. Um, and you know, it's just, just north of Ashburton. So, I, I think, um, y- th- it's, it's, it's business led, it's market led, but you know, uh, protein producers, be it dairy, be it meat, have to operate within environmental limitations.

And so, as do horticulturalists. And, and so I do believe we have, we have the opportunity for an and/and with a very light footprint. Yeah. But you do recognize that land use change in that direction has certainly a climate benefit and probably a water benefit too? Um, I, I, I can see your point of view, but I will always, um, support our dairy farmers particularly in that they have as light a touch as possible on water and on the environment simply because they cannot operate if they don't.

Well, they currently can operate with a pretty intense touch. No, I disagree. I think, I think they're, they're managing their, um, footprint incredibly well. W- what about the nitrate emergency in Canterbury and the E. coli emergency for that matter?

Well, again, I have absolutely no responsibility for that, but I spoke out very strongly against it. I think it was absolutely ludicrous, mischievous move by some departing councilors leaving EICAN that served no purpose other than to, um, to have a crack at dairy farmers in Canterbury.

Do you think it's mi- mischievous for pregnant women to be concerned that at five milligrams nitrate water could be increasing the potential of them having a preterm birth by 47%? No, I don't. But if you look to Ministry of Health data in the last 20 years, just two babies have presented to hospitals across this country- Mm-hmm.

... with what might be considered nitrate poisoning. It has not been confirmed. That's blue baby syndrome. But also the Ministry of Health advised in the previous government that pregnant women shouldn't drink high nitrate drinking water because it risks deoxygenating the baby's blood- And if they are drinking public supply, they won't be drinking high nitrate water. If they are drinking off private supply, they should probably be getting their water checked.

The Gore town exceeded the maximum acceptable value this year. Yes. And they got caught up on it very quickly. And was above five milligrams, which is the level at which there is a risk to preterm birth, which is different from the blue baby syndrome. It got caught up on it very quickly. Do you think there's a responsibility to warn people of that health risk?

Yeah, I do. But that was Gore. You asked me about EICAN. Yeah, I can be fair. Yeah. Well, EICAN is also a risk over time. Well, uh, EICAN tend to test boars that have had problems for 30 years. So this is not a new problem. But it's worsening. Depends on whose science you're following.

The, all, all the science (laughs) . (laughs) I mean all the independent science. I think with all due respect, this has kind of deviated away from my ministerial responsibility. Sure. But I appreciate you, uh, allowing us to do that. (laughs) I have one very final question for you, Minister, um, that just popped into my mind before you were having your conversation over there.

Um, so a lot of the growers, particularly those in hot, hot houses, uh, (00:08:05) glasshouses, that's, are captured in the ETS, um, and, uh, had... I've had conversations with a grower in particular, a cucumber grower, I think, from Kaiapoi who, um, the cost for him to... 'cause it's a small family business- Yep.

... um, to move away from what he is using traditionally now to, I guess, heat the, um- Coal. Yeah. I don't know if it's coal or whatever it is, but, um- (laughs)... has there been any thinking or any work going on, um, in how, um, government might support, uh, growers, the likes of those sorts of growers to move to that cleaner, um, form of heating that's not gonna break them?

Well, look, it's, um, a lot of these growers are the unfortunate victim of, of decisions that have been made by previous governments- Mm-hmm. ... um, where there is a real insecurity in energy supply now. Um, and yes, I have visited a number of them that are extremely stressed about the cost of energy- Yeah.

... and the cost of running their business, and also the cost of transitioning. And a lot of them are using either coal-fired or diesel- Yeah.... powered heating systems. Mm-hmm. Um, and so we are... I think just recently you and Todd McClay just went up to meet with a number of them, um, to talk about how we can help enable that transition.

But, um, you know, to be very blunt, there won't be government funding invested in individual businesses to transition- Not like the Giddy Fund? Something like the Giddy Fund, There's no longer a Giddy Fund. No, I know, there's not th- no longer, but something a little better. But thank you. Thank you. Very good. Thank you, Minister. It's done.

Thank you, We're actually one minute over time, so there you go. (laughs) I needed to make up as well. Two minutes over time. Thank you everyone. Appreciate the time. Thank you, Minister. Thanks, Ray. See you tomorrow? (laughs) (laughs) Well, you won't be sleeping. (laughs) (laughs)
